elements: 3D object A in a fixed starting orientation
3D object B in a rotated end orientation
transparent connecting path indicating rotation axis and angle
angle annotations showing degree of rotation between A and B

interactions.teaches: Directly manipulate object B to see the continuous transformation and understand the spatial rotation needed to align with object A

demo_steps.title: Initial alignment
demo_steps.narration: Here we have two identical objects, A and B. Object A is fixed, while you can rotate object B to understand how it needs to rotate to match object A. Notice that B starts in a different orientation.

demo_steps.title: Rotating object B
demo_steps.narration: Slowly rotate object B along the indicated path. As you adjust the angle, the visual representation of the rotation becomes clearer. Observe how the shape orientation changes until it matches with object A.

demo_steps.title: Complete alignment
demo_steps.narration: When you reach a 90-degree rotation, notice that object B perfectly aligns with A. This demonstrates how mental rotation transforms an object in space from one viewpoint to another.

plan.camera_suggestion: Orthographic view focused closely on both objects to show subtle details, with minimal perspective distortion. The camera should be positioned to offer a clear frontal view where initial misalignment is evident.

inline_constraints.default_view: must match the original image silhouette, size, label scale, and crop
inline_constraints.visible_ui: none exceeding the interactive object space; controls are embedded unobtrusively
inline_constraints.explainers: hover tooltips and click popups only
inline_constraints.framing: match the source crop and whitespace; do not force-fill the iframe if the original has margins
inline_constraints.camera: infer the source camera angle/projection/zoom so the first rendered frame is a drop-in visual replacement

plan.notes: Use Three.js to ensure smooth drag interactions on the object's surface while reflecting precise rotational movement. Maintain object fidelity to ensure alignment with original geometric details.

plan.contextChunk:
many computer vision object recognition systems
@Felzenszwalb2010, @Fischler1973, @Weber2000.

### Invariances

Object recognition in humans seems to be quite robust to changes in
viewpoint, illumination, occlusion, deformations, styles, and so on.
However, perception is not completely invariant to those variables.

One well studied property of the human visual system is its invariance
to image and 3D rotations. As we discussed in chapter
@sec-bias_and_shift, studies in human visual perception
have shown that objects are recognized faster when they appear in
canonical poses. In a landmark study, Roger N. Shepard and Jaqueline
Metzler @Shepard1971aa showed to participants pairs of drawings of
three-dimensional objects (@fig-mental_rotation). The pair of drawings
could show the same object with a mirror or the same object with an
arbitrary 3D rotation. The task consisted in deciding if the two objects
were identical (up to a 3D rotation) or mirror images of each other.
During the experiment, they recorded the success rate and the reaction
time (how long did participant took to answer). The result showed that
participants had a reaction time proportional to the rotation angle
between the two views. This supported the idea that participants were
performing a **mental rotation** in order to compare both views. Whether
humans actually perform mental rotations or not still remains
controversial.

![Two figures related by a 3D rotation. Modified from @Shepard1971aa](figures/object_recognition/mental_rotation.png){width="60%" #fig-mental_rotation}

Michael Tarr and Steven Pinker @Tarr1989MentalRA showed that similar
results where obtained on a learning task. When observers learn to
recognize a novel object from a single viewpoint they are able to
generalize to new viewpoints but they do that at a cost: recognition
time increases with the angle of rotation as if they had to mentally
rotate the object in order to compare it with the view viewed during
training. When trained with multiple viewpoints, participants recognized
equally quickly all the familiar orientations.

The field of cognitive science has formulated a number hypothesis about
the mechanisms used for recognizing objects: geometry based models,
view-based templates, prototypes, and so on. Many of those hypothesis
have been the inspiration for computer vision approaches for object
recognition.

elements: axes of rotation for each shape
connecting line indicating comparison or relationship between shapes

interactions.teaches: Manually rotating the left shape helps demonstrate how different angles affect perception and recognition
Manually rotating the right shape allows learners to test their ability to recognize rotations as being the same object from different angles

interactions.label: Show Axes
interactions.teaches: Toggling the axes provides insight into how the objects can be mentally rotated for comparison

demo_steps.title: Initial Object Display
demo_steps.narration: Here you see two geometric shapes. We're exploring the concept of mental rotation. In this step, the objects are shown in a default pose which you can recognize quickly.

demo_steps.title: Rotate the Left Shape
demo_steps.narration: Attempt to rotate the left shape. Notice how as the angle becomes more extreme, it takes more effort to mentally map this shape onto the right one.

demo_steps.title: Axes Reveal
demo_steps.narration: Toggle the axes on to better understand rotation. This helps visualize how one shape needs to be oriented to match the other and can mimic mental rotation.

demo_steps.title: Rotate the Right Shape
demo_steps.narration: Now try rotating the right shape. By doing this, challenge yourself to recognize if the shapes are identical or mirror images. The mental effort required reflects the concepts discussed.

demo_steps.title: Alignment Challenge
demo_steps.narration: Finish by aligning both shapes. Successfully matching them signifies a mental rotation completion. This practice helps understand how orientation affects visual recognition.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Front-facing view of both shapes centered in frame, allowing easy visualization of rotations without extreme perspectives
interactionPlan.notes: Use smooth animations for rotations to maintain visual continuity and aid in the cognitive task of mental rotation.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: Generated figure is positioned at a different angle compared to the source.
Additional red and blue lines are present, which do not appear in the source.
The shapes do not have the same spatial arrangement or alignment as the source.
There is an unnecessary checkbox for showing axes that does not contribute to pedagogical value.
The number of cubes and their orientation differ between source and generated figure.
evaluation.notes: The generated figure has incorrect orientation, extra elements not in the source, and lacks meaningful interaction.
action_items: Adjust the orientation and positions of all shapes to match the source image.
Remove the red and blue lines as they do not exist in the source.
Ensure the cube arrangement exactly mirrors the source, fixing discrepancies in spatial layout.
Remove the unnecessary checkbox and ensure any interactions are meaningful.
Recreate the scene without adding extra elements not in the reference image.

feedback.rationale: The presence of Wrong-Primitives and Hallucination failure modes, along with low scores and extra, incorrect elements, indicate the conceptual decomposition and interaction plan are flawed and need revision.

interactions.teaches: Manually rotating the left shape helps explore the concept of mental rotation, demonstrating how we perceive rotated objects
Rotating the right shape allows direct comparison to test if the two shapes are identical through mental rotation

demo_steps.title: Observe the Default Pose
demo_steps.narration: Here, you see both geometric shapes in their default poses. This step is about understanding their initial configuration before exploring rotations.

demo_steps.title: Rotate the Left Shape
demo_steps.narration: Try rotating the left shape. Notice as the angle increases, your mind struggles more to map it onto the right shape — demonstrating the mental rotation needed.

demo_steps.title: Attempt to Match
demo_steps.narration: Start rotating the right shape to see if you can align both shapes identically. This interaction highlights our ability to recognize objects through mental manipulation.

demo_steps.title: Challenge Your Perception
demo_steps.narration: Try extreme rotations on both shapes. As difficulty increases, it shows how the mental rotation process becomes more complex with larger angles.

plan.camera_suggestion: Standard orthographic view to ensure clear visibility of rotations. Center both shapes in the frame for easy side-by-side comparison.
plan.notes: Ensure that any rendered transformations align with the source image's spatial relationships. Keep the interactions seamless and transitions smooth to better reflect the cognitive process.
